tactics: For CWE-79 (Cross-Site Scripting): When a user-controlled value is embedded in HTML/JavaScript output, verify whether the sink applies contextual escaping (e.g., HTML entity encoding, JavaScript string escaping) or sanitization. Do not dismiss the finding solely because the input appears to come from an internal source or a non-obvious user-controlled path; trace the full data flow to confirm if untrusted data can reach the sink without proper encoding.

For CWE-22 (Path Traversal): When a file path is constructed using user-controlled input, check if the input is validated against a whitelist of allowed characters or a restricted set of base directories, not just whether it contains dot-dot-slash patterns. Also verify that any canonicalization or path normalization occurs before the check, as missing normalization can allow bypasses that make the validation ineffective.

For CWE-200 (Information Disclosure): When a finding flags exposure of sensitive data (e.g., in error messages, logs, or responses), confirm whether the exposed data is truly sensitive (e.g., credentials, PII, session tokens) and whether the exposure is to an untrusted context. Do not classify as a true positive if the data is already public, intentionally exposed to the user, or is non-sensitive internal state that does not aid an attacker.

For CWE-502 (Deserialization of Untrusted Data): When deserialization is flagged, verify whether the serialized data originates from an untrusted source (e.g., network, user upload) and whether the deserialization mechanism allows arbitrary type instantiation or has known gadget chains. If the data is from a trusted internal source or the deserializer is safely configured with a strict type whitelist, the finding is likely benign.

For CWE-89 (SQL Injection): When a SQL query is built dynamically, check if the user-controlled input is incorporated using parameterized queries (e.g., PreparedStatement, bound parameters) or an ORM that safely escapes values. Do not assume a finding is benign just because the input is validated with a regex or sanitized with string replacements; such defenses are often bypassable and do not guarantee safety like parameterization.

For CWE-78 (OS Command Injection): When user input is passed to a command execution function, verify if the input is strictly validated against a whitelist of safe values or if the command is invoked without shell interpretation (e.g., using an array form that avoids shell metacharacter expansion). Do not rely solely on blacklist filtering of special characters, as bypasses are common.
